elements.sent-2411-516: Different rationales of construction can be identified, that are primarily related to the uses of space, their evolution, or, on the contrary, their persistence and stability.
elements.sent-2411-517: Depending on the neighborhoods, certain elements are predominantly used by the owners to locate and identify their properties, occupying a central place in the construction of individuals' lived spaces.
elements.sent-2411-518: In the old intra-muros of the city, that of the 13\textsuperscript{th century, it is often the main roads of the city, those that have structured the city since antiquity.}
elements.sent-2411-519: These are main arteries that traverse the neighborhood, punctuating exchanges and social interactions.
elements.sent-2411-520: Many communities are built around these streets, though not all of these communities have the same characteristics.
elements.sent-2411-521: While some communities are more residential, others are resolutely oriented towards craftsmanship and commerce, or even towards accommodating travelers.
elements.sent-2411-522: The places that unite them are often revealing of their typology.
elements.sent-2411-523: Given its location near the Pont Saint-Bénézet and the Rhône river, C21, for example, has many inns, which are significant social places and important topographical landmarks, often with very conspicuous signs.
elements.sent-2411-524: Like the inns, the bathhouses are places of social interaction and notable landmarks in a neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-525: Alongside the most structuring streets of the community and the inns, it is thus a bathhouse that holds one of the highest degrees in this community.
elements.sent-2411-526: Neighborhoods with a much more residential aspect than the one we just mentioned also aggregate around many roads.
elements.sent-2411-527: Community C5 is a good example.
elements.sent-2411-528: Rue des Ortolans plays an important role in the life of the neighborhood and in the perception the owners have of it.
elements.sent-2411-529: It has long been dominated by the presence of a prominent Avignonese family, who gave its name to the street and who settled in the city well before the arrival of the popes.
elements.sent-2411-530: The residential character of this neighborhood is notably based on the fact that it is a recently constructed area, at least more recent than the heart of the city further north.

elements.sent-2411-531: A bit further north of C5, community detection reveals a particularly imposing neighborhood both in the number of nodes it contains and its extent (C1).
elements.sent-2411-532: It has an obvious residential character and includes notably beautiful and large residences.
elements.sent-2411-533: Until 1370, one house is particularly used by contemporaries to situate themselves in this area: it is that of Tymburge Vayrane, a property owner with a rich real estate portfolio throughout the city.
elements.sent-2411-534: This house is mentioned as a landmark more often than some streets.
elements.sent-2411-535: This illustrates how much the neighborhood and social interactions directly influence the perception of the users.
elements.sent-2411-536: Like the ones we just mentioned, C8 is a very good example of the centrality of streets in the composition and definition of neighborhoods.
elements.sent-2411-537: The community has a relatively small diameter (8) given the number of its nodes (126).
elements.sent-2411-538: Spatially speaking, it is particularly dense, which is quite revealing of the very genesis of the neighborhood and its development from the 11\textsuperscript{th century.}
elements.sent-2411-539: The extent of this community corresponds very likely to that of the bishop's boroughs called Scofaria and Pelliparia.
elements.sent-2411-540: The regularity of the plots around the churches, especially to the south of the main street, suggests that the bishop, holder of the borough, had certainly intervened very precisely in the subdivision of certain parts of the area.
elements.sent-2411-541: Community C18 has a very different characteristic, much less residential.
elements.sent-2411-542: It is located in the heart of the city, spanning several parishes.
elements.sent-2411-543: It is built around the most important and oldest commercial areas and marketplaces of the city.
elements.sent-2411-544: This neighborhood is resolutely commercial, and this is particularly evident in the profile of the individuals one encounters and the types of properties they own.
elements.sent-2411-545: Thus, in addition to their houses, the owners declare numerous shops or stalls, two types of properties not found in more residential neighborhoods like C5.
elements.sent-2411-546: Conversely, certain features of residences found in other neighborhoods are completely absent here: there are no gardens or courtyards; the space is primarily used by and for commerce.
elements.sent-2411-547: If the streets are at the heart of neighborhood construction, it is primarily because they are the sites of social interactions.
elements.sent-2411-548: These streets are organized around a significant and unifying household, host commercial and artisanal activities, and are central to the city's political representation.
elements.sent-2411-549: Each neighborhood also has essential elements for the daily lives of individuals, such as wells, ovens, and cemeteries, which contribute to the construction of individuals' perception of places.
elements.sent-2411-550: These are undeniable landmarks of the urban landscape, places of sociability, exchanges, and meetings.
elements.sent-2411-551: As such, they are often the vertices with the highest degree in communities.
elements.sent-2411-552: In the city, burial sites are thus privileged and central spaces for exchanges and sociability.
elements.sent-2411-553: They must also be associated with public places such as streets or even more so, squares.
elements.sent-2411-554: Community 13 is a very good example.
elements.sent-2411-555: It is very small and particularly concentrated (31 vertices for a diameter of 3).
elements.sent-2411-556: All these properties are connected to the Saint-Symphorien cemetery.
elements.sent-2411-557: The degree of centralization of the community is, in this respect, quite high.
elements.sent-2411-558: Here, the cemetery is the spatial reference par excellence, and the neighborhood is entirely built around the burial site.
elements.sent-2411-559: Within the old walls, communities are thus constructed by the proximity relationship between properties and individuals and by the presence of topographical landmarks that shape the perception and usage of individuals in the neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-560: The neighborhoods located outside the walls of the 13\textsuperscript{th century follow very diverse logics, which differ in several ways from the communities studied so far.}
elements.sent-2411-561: The largest neighborhood detected in this area corresponds to C2.
elements.sent-2411-562: At first glance, it might seem to follow the same construction logics as those located within the old walls.
elements.sent-2411-563: However, it has certain characteristics specific to its location and its distance from the historical center of the city.
elements.sent-2411-564: To locate their properties, the owners refer not only to the streets but also to recent urbanization: the numerous boroughs developed in this area on former agricultural land.
elements.sent-2411-565: Alongside these boroughs, many gardens are still present.
elements.sent-2411-566: People frequently use these gardens as reference points.
elements.sent-2411-567: This neighborhood has another unique characteristic: in addition to its semi-rural aspect, created by the number of gardens adjoining the new houses of the boroughs, it houses the dependencies of many services from the old intra-muros.
elements.sent-2411-568: It is likely that this community also hosts the pleasure homes of certain prelates, as suggested by the number of residences described as large and beautiful, accompanied by gardens, courtyards, or wells.
elements.sent-2411-569: This neighborhood also has a significant presence of artisans and merchants.
elements.sent-2411-570: The presence of large gardens still cultivated at the end of the 14\textsuperscript{th century must have given a rural appearance to the place, sharply contrasting with the old intra-muros, which was very densely built and populated.}
elements.sent-2411-571: As such, it is overall not densely built.
elements.sent-2411-572: Two other communities (C19 and C30), located outside the 13extsuperscript{th century walls, share characteristics with C2 and could thus be described as semi-rural neighborhoods.}
elements.sent-2411-573: They retain the marks of significant rurality while being partly occupied by recent urbanization in certain areas.
elements.sent-2411-574: Finally, a specific typology of community outside the 13extsuperscript{th century walls must be highlighted.}
elements.sent-2411-575: These are what could be called the borough communities, such as C15.
elements.sent-2411-576: Here, the borough forms the neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-577: This is quite noticeable when looking at the elements to which individuals refer to situate themselves in this space.
elements.sent-2411-578: Firstly, it is the straight street of the borough that is used: it unites the community built around it, suggesting the planning of the road in the borough's subdivision project.
elements.sent-2411-579: Although less used, another street is mentioned; it is called the ``vicinal street'' of the borough; there is also a cross street, a dead-end, and a square.
elements.sent-2411-580: In all cases, the attachment of these elements to the borough is specified.
elements.sent-2411-581: These details remind us that while these streets are not structuring at the city level, they are strongly so at the neighborhood and borough level.
elements.sent-2411-582: Whereas the boundaries of the previously studied neighborhoods were more difficult to perceive, especially for those within the 13\textsuperscript{th century walls, here the neighborhood space is clearly delineated.}
elements.sent-2411-583: It is no longer just a perceived, subjective space with fuzzy boundaries, constructed by the proximity and sociability of individuals around various places and inherited spaces.
elements.sent-2411-584: In the case of the "borough neighborhood," the boundaries are tangible and inherent to the very genesis of the place.
elements.sent-2411-585: Proximity and sociability are indeed present, as evidenced by the presence of a large borough well to which the owners sometimes refer.
elements.sent-2411-586: However, the neighborhood is built here around a predefined space, not an unplanned process inherited from an "already existing space." The typology of the declared properties once again highlights this, forming a particularly homogeneous neighborhood.
elements.sent-2411-587: The graph extracted using our method is a sufficiently good approximation of spatial distance for the communities to correspond to spatial segments representative of real neighborhoods in the sense of lived space.
elements.sent-2411-588: In this regard, the detection of communities in the graph shows a partition of the city consistent with social practices.
elements.sent-2411-589: The elements that define the communities are multiple and complementary (livability, buildings, neighborhoods, commerce) and they are essentially places of sociability or symbolic markers of the urban landscape.
elements.sent-2411-590: They pace and define spatial perceptions.
elements.sent-2411-591: These perceptions are primarily based on the layering of heritages, with evolutions being integrated only slowly.
elements.sent-2411-592: In this article, we tackled the problem of segmenting a historical urban space based on incomplete data.
elements.sent-2411-593: We focused on the specific case of medieval Avignon, during the papacy.
elements.sent-2411-594: On the one hand, historical sources rarely provide the exact locations of the buildings constituting the city, but on the other hand, they describe how they are located relative to each other.
elements.sent-2411-595: We adopted a graph-based approach to take advantage of this relational information while modeling the city, and proposed several methods to extract such confront networks.
elements.sent-2411-596: We designed two conflicting objective criteria to select the optimal extraction method: data coverage and distance correlation.
elements.sent-2411-597: It turns out the best method requires discarding some of the information provided by the original historical sources in order to preserve the spatial proximity encoded in the graph.
elements.sent-2411-598: Moreover, our results show that leveraging certain information from secondary sources can considerably improve the quality of the graph.
elements.sent-2411-599: Finally, we partitioned the best confront network through community detection, in order to perform a qualitative analysis of the resulting segmentation of the urban space.
elements.sent-2411-600: Our discussion showed that the communities are completely consistent with historical knowledge, and help understand how people perceived urban space at the time.
elements.sent-2411-601: We identify mainly two perspectives to extend our work.
elements.sent-2411-602: First, the land registries that we used in this article are very common in historical studies, especially during the Medieval Ages.
elements.sent-2411-603: However, they do not always take exactly the same form, and can also vary in content from one city to the other, depending on the local practices.
elements.sent-2411-604: We want to assess the robustness of our extraction approach by applying it to other cities, which will require some adaptation.
elements.sent-2411-605: For example, in the case of the city of Orleans~\cite{Fianu2011 , the spatial relationships partly differ from those described in Section~\ref{sec:DataRelations and Table~\ref{tab:ConvRel for Avignon: there are only very few cardinal relationships.}}}
elements.sent-2411-606: But this could be compensated by leveraging other types of information, for instance regarding the notaries and families involved, and their social relationships.
elements.sent-2411-607: The second perspective concerns the estimation of the missing absolute positions.
elements.sent-2411-608: In the case of Avignon, the position of certain spatial objects is known with reasonable certainty, and can be leveraged together with the spatial relationships encoded in the confront networks, to interpolate the position of the other objects.
elements.sent-2411-609: A simple baseline consists in averaging the position of the neighboring objects, but this does not account for the semantics of the spatial relationships.
elements.sent-2411-610: We want to take advantage of Graph Neural Networks~\cite{Hamilton2020 to perform a better prediction, possibly also using the many vertex attributes at our disposal.}
elements.sent-2411-611: The source code written to implement our extraction methods and experiments is available online at \url{https://github.com/CompNet/MedievalAvignon .}
